summary: Uncle Bob uses a Socratic dialogue to push back against the notion that Design Patterns are outdated relics of the 1990s. He argues that while frameworks like Rails, Angular, and Node may come and go as 'glitter,' the underlying Design Patterns they embody are timeless. The post emphasizes that patterns provide a shared vocabulary and canonical forms that allow professional developers to communicate efficiently and write recognizable code. He concludes by listing all 23 patterns from the Gang of Four book, making clear that modern developers still need to learn them.
key_points: The Design Patterns book remains one of the most important software books of the last 20+ years despite its age
Frameworks are 'glitter' that sparkles and fades, but the design patterns within them are enduring ideas
Design Patterns provide named, canonical forms that enable efficient communication between professional developers
The fundamentals of software have changed very little in the last four decades
Knowing patterns saves time because designers can communicate using shared names rather than explaining concepts from scratch
money_quotes: The Design Patterns book is one of the most important books, if not THE most important book, written about software within the last 20 years.
The fundamentals of software have changed very little in the last four decades.
The ideas aren't glitter. The frameworks are. Most of the ideas in those frameworks are old. They've been around for decades. The ideas are… Design Patterns.
A Design Pattern is a named canonical form for a combination of software structures and procedures that have proven to be useful over the years.
By following the canonical form, you relieve everyone else of the work of having to decode what you have done.
Social Networks: The font of all wisdom and knowledge.
key_insight: Trendy frameworks come and go, but the Design Patterns underlying them are timeless fundamentals that every professional developer must learn.
